steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the main purpose of the text. The passage introduces a feature of the Aubrey/Maturin series (abrupt endings), then presents two opposing critical reactions to it. The correct answer must capture both the feature and the disagreement.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — To present a reason the unusual structure that O'Brian uses for *The Hundred Days* makes it one of his most complex books.: The word "complex" never appears and neither book is ranked against the others. The passage does not argue complexity. ✗
  • Choice B — To argue that the Aubrey/Maturin series should have the literary renown of *In Search of Lost Time*.: Proust is mentioned only as an analogy for a multivolume work, not as a benchmark the series should reach. ✗
  • Choice C — To describe a characteristic of the Aubrey/Maturin novels and offer two differing viewpoints on this characteristic.: Matches perfectly: the abrupt endings are the characteristic, and the word "But" signals the pivot to the second viewpoint. ✓
  • Choice D — To explain why many critics find the Aubrey/Maturin novels to be remarkably entertaining despite flaws in the novels' structures.: Entertainment value is never mentioned. The second critic denies the endings are flaws, so "despite flaws" misrepresents the passage. ✗
That matches Choice C — To describe a characteristic of the Aubrey/Maturin novels and offer two differing viewpoints on this characteristic..
